question: For the `Point` class (from a previous question with `x`, `y` coordinates), overload the `==` operator to compare if two `Point` objects have the same coordinates. It should return a boolean value.

test_cases.input: Point p1(1, 2); Point p2(1, 2); p1 == p2;
Point p1(1, 2); Point p3(3, 4); p1 == p3;
Point p4(5, 6); Point p5(5, 7); p4 == p5;
Point p6(8, 8); Point p7(8, 8); p6 == p7;

driver: Point p1(1, 2); Point p2(1, 2); Point p3(3, 4); std::cout << (p1 == p2 ? "true" : "false") << std::endl; std::cout << (p1 == p3 ? "true" : "false") << std::endl; Point p4(5, 6); Point p5(5, 7); std::cout << (p4 == p5 ? "true" : "false") << std::endl; Point p6(8, 8); Point p7(8, 8); std::cout << (p6 == p7 ? "true" : "false") << std::endl; std::cout << (Point(10, 20) == Point(10, 20) ? "true" : "false") << std::endl;
errors: The `operator==` passes `Point` objects by value instead of `const` reference, leading to unnecessary object copying and reduced efficiency. The logic is also overly verbose with nested `if/else` statements, which can be simplified into a single boolean expression for improved readability.
